items.q: Why does longshore drift move sediment in only one direction along a coast?
items.correct: The prevailing wind drives swash diagonally up the beach at an angle, but gravity-driven backwash returns sediment straight downhill — creating net movement in the swash direction.
items.misconception: Longshore drift moves sediment back and forth with the tide.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift only happens during storms.
items.why: It's wave-driven, not tide-driven. The net direction is set by the prevailing wind angle relative to the coast — that's why groynes are placed perpendicular to interrupt it.

items.q: What is the sequence of headland erosion from crack to stump?
items.correct: Crack to cave to arch to stack to stump — waves widen cracks into caves, two caves meet to form an arch, the arch collapses leaving a stack, and the stack erodes to a stump.
items.misconception: Stacks form where rocks fall from the cliff into the sea.
items.random_wrong: Stacks form when the sea level rises and floods a hill.
items.why: It's a sequence of erosion at the same site, not random rockfall or sea level rise. Each landform is the predecessor of the next, with hydraulic action and abrasion driving the progression.

items.q: What is lag time on a storm hydrograph?
items.correct: The time between peak rainfall and peak river discharge — affected by drainage basin shape, land use, soil saturation and vegetation.
items.misconception: Lag time is how long the storm itself lasts.
items.random_wrong: Lag time is the delay between thunder and lightning.
items.why: Students misread the graph. Lag time is the gap between maximum rain and maximum river flow — it tells you how quickly the basin responds, not the duration of rainfall.

items.q: Which is NOT one of the four pillars of food security?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Production efficiency. “Availability” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which UN agency leads on food and agriculture?
items.why: Correct answer: FAO. “WHO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate global daily dietary energy supply per person (kcal)?
items.why: The real figure is 2900. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
